quick_facts.label: Role scope
quick_facts.value: A Travel Consultant plans and finalises tour packages and itineraries, arranges travel insurance and visas, and supports customers during tours.

quick_facts.label: National skills standard
quick_facts.value: The Tourism & Hospitality Skill Council qualification pack identifies the role as Travel Consultant, QP code THC/Q4404, in the Tours and Travels sub-sector and Tour Packaging occupation at NSQF Level 4.5.

quick_facts.label: Typical services
quick_facts.value: India’s Ministry of Tourism defines a travel agent as arranging tickets for air, rail or ship travel and passport/visa services, and potentially arranging accommodation, tours, entertainment and related tourism services.

quick_facts.label: Indicative progression
quick_facts.value: The National Career Service lists an indicative path from Travel Agent to Retail Travel Manager and then Corporate Travel Consultant; actual titles and progression differ by employer and segment.

sections.heading: What a Travel Consultant does
paragraphs: A Travel Consultant translates a client’s purpose, budget, dates and preferences into a workable itinerary and tour package. The current Tourism & Hospitality Skill Council (THSC) qualification pack specifically covers itinerary and package preparation, travel-risk assessment and insurance arrangements, visa support, customer assistance during the tour, communication/service standards, confidentiality and guest privacy, and workplace safety.

The work therefore combines customer advisory and sales activity with detailed operational coordination: confirming transport and accommodation arrangements, explaining conditions and schedules, preparing or checking supporting documents, and responding when a traveller needs assistance. The Ministry of Tourism’s travel-agent definition similarly includes tickets, passport and visa arrangements, accommodation, tours and related tourism services.
bullets: Consult with travellers, gather requirements and propose suitable destinations, routes and packages.
Prepare costed itineraries and coordinate reservations or supplier services.
Assist with visa-related processes and travel-insurance arrangements within the organisation’s service scope.
Provide pre-departure information and help customers resolve issues during travel.
Handle customer information carefully and maintain service, hygiene and safety standards.

sections.heading: Entry routes after BTTM
paragraphs: A Bachelor of Tourism and Travel Management (BTTM) is a directly relevant foundation for this role because BTTM curricula cover travel-agency and tour-operations business alongside tourism principles, management and communication. A BTTM graduate can target entry-level roles such as travel consultant, reservations/ticketing executive, tour-packaging assistant or travel-desk executive, then develop through client handling and supplier coordination experience.

A skills-based route is also available. THSC’s Travel Consultant qualification pack (THC/Q4404) defines an NSQF Level 4.5 role and is accompanied by a model curriculum. Learners should verify the current training provider, assessment and certification arrangements before enrolling, since skills qualifications and implementation arrangements can be revised.

For practical readiness, prioritise internships, placements or supervised work in travel agencies, tour operators, online travel businesses, corporate travel desks or destination-management firms. University tourism programmes also describe travel-agency operations, travel documents, central reservation systems and global distribution systems as relevant operational learning areas.
bullets: Complete BTTM and use internships or field projects to demonstrate itinerary design, customer service and supplier coordination.
Build job-ready capability in reservation workflows, travel documentation, package costing and written client communication.
Consider an NSQF-aligned Travel Consultant course where it provides credible practical training and assessment.
Apply for junior consultant, ticketing/reservations, tour operations, visa-assistance or travel-desk roles, depending on the employer’s service mix.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The THSC standard emphasises a service-oriented approach, politeness, effective communication, attention to detail and the ability to work long hours when required. These are important because a small error in names, dates, routing, documents or booking conditions can affect a customer’s journey.

Technical competence should include itinerary construction, package finalisation, visa and insurance workflows, travel-risk awareness, and careful handling of confidential guest information. The National Career Service also identifies interpersonal, problem-solving and excellent communication skills as relevant to the closely aligned Travel Agent occupation.

Digital operational skills are increasingly useful. Tourism programmes identify central reservation systems (CRS) and global distribution systems (GDS) as travel-business tools; employers may additionally train recruits on their own booking platforms, CRM systems and supplier portals.
bullets: Clear written and spoken communication, including explaining fare, accommodation and document conditions accurately.
Attention to detail, time management and systematic checking of booking and traveller data.
Customer service, empathy and problem solving during disruptions or changes.
Itinerary design, package costing and basic knowledge of transport, accommodation and destination products.
Visa-document, insurance and privacy-awareness practices.
Reservation and distribution-system familiarity, developed through coursework, training or employer onboarding.

sections.heading: Where the role is performed
paragraphs: Travel Consultants commonly work in travel agencies and related tourism businesses. The National Career Service characterises the related Travel Agent role as desk-based, while the Ministry of Tourism and the Incredible India travel-partner directory show that travel agents may offer ticketing, transport, document processing, language support and other travel services.

The day-to-day workplace can therefore be an agency office, tour operator, corporate travel desk, online travel business or a destination-management setting. Work may include client calls and digital booking work, with occasional supplier, client or tour-support coordination depending on the business model.
bullets: Travel agencies and tour operators.
Corporate travel and travel-desk operations.
Online travel and reservation-led businesses.
Destination-management and specialist travel providers, including firms focused on ticketing or document processing.

sections.heading: Career development
paragraphs: Early career development usually comes from taking on more complex itineraries, higher-value clients, specialist destinations, group travel, corporate accounts or operational responsibility. The National Career Service gives an indicative progression of Travel Agent to Retail Travel Manager to Corporate Travel Consultant, but employers may use different titles such as senior travel consultant, tour executive, reservations lead or account manager.

A BTTM graduate can strengthen progression prospects by documenting measurable work outcomes: booking accuracy, customer satisfaction, turnaround time, conversion of enquiries to bookings, supplier coordination and successful handling of itinerary changes. Further tourism-management study may be useful for people moving towards management, operations or entrepreneurship; for example, IGNOU’s Master of Tourism and Travel Management is aimed at people seeking managerial tourism roles or operating a tourism enterprise.
bullets: Build breadth in domestic, inbound, outbound, leisure or corporate travel before specialising.
Develop supplier, destination and compliance knowledge alongside client-service capability.
Move towards team leadership, retail/corporate travel management, tour operations or an independent travel enterprise as experience grows.
Treat job-title pathways as indicative rather than guaranteed, because responsibilities vary between agencies, OTAs and corporate travel firms.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation varies materially by city, employer type, sales incentives, shift pattern, language skills, ticketing or GDS expertise, client segment and experience. As a dated reference only, the National Career Service page for the related Travel Agent occupation—last updated December 29, 2023—listed ₹14,500 to ₹32,000 per month for candidates with experience and expressly described the figures as indicative and subject to change. It should not be treated as a current salary benchmark or an offer expectation.

When comparing roles, assess fixed pay separately from incentives, travel benefits, targets, shift allowances and training support. Ask employers whether the role is primarily leisure sales, corporate travel servicing, ticketing/reservations, visa assistance or tour operations, since the duties and variable-pay structure can differ.
bullets: Use dated published ranges only as background context, not as a present-day market quote.
Compare total compensation and role scope, including sales targets and incentive rules.
Verify current pay directly with employers and current job advertisements for the relevant city and travel segment.
